narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your strong first-year school decile of 8 (well above the average of about 5.7), a lower third-year decile of 1 (far below the typical 5.5), an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 (right around the average of 3.21), an LSAT score of 28.5 (below the average of 36.7), and your plan to attend full-time like most students—and estimated a 43% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The three most influential factors in this decision were your year 1 and year 3 grade deciles, followed by your undergraduate GPA. Your strong performance in your first year at school stood out as the top positive factor, significantly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, showing the model recognized your early potential. However, your much weaker third-year decile ranking was the biggest negative driver, strongly pulling the prediction toward failure and outweighing that early strength in the model's eyes. Your undergraduate GPA played a smaller supportive role, nudging things slightly toward a passing prediction since it aligned closely with what's typical for students in the dataset. Compared to average applicants, your trajectory from a high first-year decile to a low third-year one is unusual and concerning to the model, as it signals a potential decline that often correlates with bar struggles, even with your solid GPA and full-time plans. While your LSAT was a drag, it wasn't among the top influencers here. To move forward, focus on diagnosing and improving whatever caused that third-year dip—perhaps through targeted academic support, retaking courses, or building study habits—then reapply with updated transcripts showing recovery, and consider LSAT prep to boost your overall profile for a stronger case next time.
